title: Karenth Dworsky
text:
Dissertation Research



Wildfire fundamentally alters the forest canopy that regulates snowpack energy balance. In the Eastern Cascades, where rain-on-snow events drive some of the most dangerous flood and debris flow hazards, understanding how canopy loss changes snowmelt timing and magnitude is an urgent and open question.
My dissertation uses remote sensing, field instrumentation, and long-term streamflow records to quantify these effects across a gradient of burn severity at two fire sites in Washington State.






Site
Fire
Year
Watershed




Labor Mountain
Labor Mountain Fire
2023
Tillicum Creek


Lower Sugarloaf
Lower Sugarloaf Fire
2024
Peshastin Creek


Squilchuck Creek
—
—
Unburned control

















Topic
Key Data
Status




Paper 1
Does RAVG canopy loss predict spatial snowpack depletion during the December 2025 atmospheric river?
MODIS fSCA · Sentinel-1 SAR · Landsat dNBR · RAVG CC-5
In progress


Paper 2
Regional rain-on-snow detection across the western US
SNOTEL · MODIS · NWM
Planned


Paper 3
Historical burned vs. unburned streamflow response — Eastern Cascades
USGS 15-min streamflow · MTBS 2009–2024
Planned


Paper 4
Field instrumentation and monitoring network design
Timelapse cameras · snow stakes · tipping buckets · pressure transducers
Planned








MODIS fSCA — fractional snow cover area time series
Sentinel-1 SAR — wet snow detection
Landsat dNBR — burn severity mapping
RAVG CC-5 — canopy cover change post-fire
Google Earth Engine — cloud-based processing platform





Field instrumentation deployed at Labor Mountain and Lower Sugarloaf includes timelapse cameras, snow stakes, tipping bucket precipitation gauges, pressure transducers, and temperature/humidity loggers. Reference SNOTEL stations: Pope Ridge, Harts Pass, Rainy Pass, and Blewett Pass.

title: Karenth Dworsky
Karenth Dworsky
text:
Curriculum Vitae



Professional Hydrologist · #24-PH-05033
Steamboat Springs, CO · ORCID










PhD, Watershed Science
Colorado State University · In progress



Advisor: Dr. Steven Fassnacht



Dissertation: Post-Fire Rain-on-Snow Hydrology in the Eastern Cascades


MS / BS
[Add your degrees here]







USDA Forest Service — Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (current)
GS-12 Watershed Project Manager · BAER Coordinator · Water Rights Program Manager

Post-fire emergency watershed assessment (BAER) coordination for major fire events
Water rights administration and interstate compact compliance
NEPA compliance for aquatic and riparian resource impacts
Tribal coordination with Yakama Nation and Colville Confederated Tribes
Aquatic organism passage (AOP) replacement and floodplain restoration

USGS — Alaska Science Center
Hydrologist

Arctic watershed monitoring and streamflow analysis

U.S. Army Corps of Engineers — Albuquerque District
Water Resources Engineer

Section 404 permitting and jurisdictional determinations
Water resources planning and flood risk analysis





PhD Dissertation — Post-Fire Rain-on-Snow Hydrology, Eastern Cascades
Colorado State University, Dr. Steven Fassnacht, 2023–present

Remote sensing of post-fire snowpack depletion using MODIS fSCA, Sentinel-1 SAR, and RAVG canopy loss metrics
Field monitoring network design across burned and unburned watersheds
Historical streamflow analysis across fire-affected Methow and Chelan basin gages

Tools

Git · GitHub








Professional Hydrologist (PH #24-PH-05033) — American Institute of Hydrology
USDA Forest Service — 12+ years federal service


Full CV available upon request.
